div.p: This passage, however, does not add anything to the lineage outlined in the Transmission Report, as it remains unclear if the image of Amitāyus (Wuliangshou Fo 無量壽佛) received by Daoquan depicted this Buddha surrounded by 50 or 52 bodhisattvas. The story about Bodhisattva Wutong from Jitoumo Monastery in India reaching Sukhāvatī may just have been borrowed as a literary motif in connection with the Buddha residing in Sukhāvatī, Amitāyus or Amitābha (Emituo Fo 阿彌陀佛), respectively. The passage on Huihai receiving the image from Daoquan can first of all be counted as evidence for the belief in auspicious images of Amitāyus, and of its thriving cult under the Sui dynasty.

A systematic comparison of the earlier stone carved Transmission Report and its canonized counterpart has so far only been undertaken by Okada Ken 岡田健. 163–173. Okada’s analysis is motivated by his search for historicity. For example, he identifies the Jitou Monastery (Jitoumo Si 鷄頭摩寺) mentioned in the inscription as the Jiyuan saṃghārāma (Jiyuan sengqielan 鷄園僧伽藍) in the kingdom of Magadha that was visited by Xuanzang 玄奘 (600/602–664).See T#2053, 50: 236b1–3. Because Xuanzang nowhere mentions veneration of a miraculous image of Amitābha at this place, Okada concludes that the legend about it may have been created not in India, but in China proper. Moreover, he regrets that it is impossible to identify a monk by the name of Ming­xian (明憲 or 明獻) in historical sources, and that the same is true for Magistrate Zheng in Yu­zhou. As a result, Okada is only able to conclude that, with regard to historical facts, it is important to note that the image of Amitābha and the 50 bodhisattvas was disseminated from the capital of the Sui.

Our present approach does not treat the Transmission Report as an apt source for reconstructing the history of the iconographical motif under review. As Okada has shown, it does not serve this purpose well. We rather assume that the message of the Transmission Report emerges from its location next to the sculpted images of niche 1, and that there is a close relation between the inscription and the image, which sets the stone text apart from Daoxuan’s version.

As the above comparison of text versions has shown, the earlier stone text is not only longer than the canonized text, it also contains information not adopted by Daoxuan (in case the stone version served as a source for his compilation). This information pertains to the temporal and spatial distance covered by the image on its route from ancient India, since the time when our world first had this image, towards the east to China, when it was first known under the Han dynasty, and further on to the present-time capital of the Sui and the Tang. Obviously, this timeline serves the purpose of authenticating what is claimed in the very first sentence of the Transmission Report, namely, that the image came to China from the Western regions.

However, the initial claim of the Transmission Report relates not only to the origin of the image, but also to its particular nature, as it is an “auspicious image” (rui­xiang 瑞像). Such images are not only capable of working wonders, they are also known for developing a life of their own. Auspicious images are able to communicate with the living, usually in dreams, to make known their intentions. They react to the rise and fall of historical dynasties with auspicious and inauspicious signs in a foretelling manner. And they possess power over their place of residence. As long as people are supposed to carry them to places, they are moveable and light; on arrival, they grow heavy and become immovable to indicate their resting place. If necessary, they walk around all by themselves, or ride a boat, or float in water.The power to travel by themselves is characteristic for a particular group of auspicious images: those believed to have been produced by the Mauryan King Aśoka (r. ca. 268–232 BCE) and to have later come to China, see 182–184.

In this context, we can comprehend the particularities in the narrative of the stone text that are not found in Daoxuan’s version. It is the original image that is being carried to China by the sisiter of Kāśyapa Mātaṅga, and not a copy of it. In the same way that Wutong Bodhisattva acted as recipient of the divine image, this nun serves as its carrier. She “came holding this image” (chi ci xiang lai 持此像來), and she “took this image and returned” (jiang ci xiang er hui 将此像而歸). Coming to China and returning to India were no decisions made by the nun, but by the image. The reason for its return is given as well, namely, the distant feelings of the Chinese people.

After its return to India, the image waited for more favorable times, pondering the possibility to make known in dreams its appearance. Dreams are adequate means of communication for auspicious images, who inform the dreamer about their wishes and needs, warn about pending dangers, or scare off enemies.See, for example, Daoxuan’s story no. 28 (T#2106, 52: 419b7–c5) on the dream of Emperor Wu 武帝 (r. 502–549) of the Liang 梁 dynasty (153–154; 173; 181), and story no. 33 (T#2106, 52: 420b21–c4) about the stone image of Lingshi Monastery (Lingshi Si 靈石寺) in Jinzhou 晋州 in need of repair (161; 172; 182). In his overview of Chinese dynasties, 1–121, presents some dream incidents transmitted in literary sources that are ascribed to the first to sixth century. We also find the dream motif in the founding legend of the present Yuqun Monastery (Yuquan Si 玉泉寺; formerly known as Gushan Monastery Gushan Si 谷山寺), located north of the main summit of Mount Tai (Tai Shan 泰山) in Shandong. The legend, transmitted on the Stele with the Record on Gushan Monastery (Gushan Si ji bei 谷山寺記碑) of 1201, mentions a Luohan image that communicated in dreams, and was subsequently discovered by locals and moved to the place where the monastery was then established, see 28–31. The image of the Transmission Report needs to communicate its true appearance as the “Buddha Amitābha sitting on a lotus with a thousand leaves that all spring from the same root” (Emituo Fo zuo qianye lianhua tonggen bingchu 阿弥陁佛坐千葉蓮華，同根竝出). Although the inscription does not relate when and to whom these details were communicated, Dharma Master Ming­xian 明獻 receives “such an image” (ci yi qu 此一軀) at the earliest possible date, namely, in the first year of the kaihuang era, which marks the establishment of the Buddhist reign of the first Sui emperor.

Henceforth, the sculpted image is disseminated throughout China by crafted copies of the original. But these crafted copies are all certified as being “not different from the original.” As proof of authentication, they are transmitted together with the “original record” (benzhuan 本傳).

Summing up this evidence, the acting agents of the Transmission Report are not so much the personalities who come into contact with the image and hold it in their hands, but the image itself. Therefore, it seems justified to read the word zhuan 傳 in the inscription title not just as a “report” about how the image was transmitted to China and Mount Wolong, but also as the biography or hagiography of the image. In analogy to hagiographies collected in such works as the Biographies of Eminent Monks (Gaoseng zhuan 高僧傳) and its sequels, we are confronted here with the life story of an auspicious image, informing us about its coming into the world, its travels through the world, and the goals it pursued by doing so.

Reading the Transmission Report as an hagiographical account of the image of Amitābha and the 50 bodhisattvas, we thus realize that this image possesses powers comparable to those of Buddha relics. This impression is reinforced by the fact that the image of Amitābha and the 50 bodhisattvas was not created by an artist, but came into being by an act of bestowal. Both versions, the stone text, and the canonized story, underline this aspect of the image. Amitābha Buddha may have allowed the bodhisattva Wutong to picture his “true appearance,” or he may have assisted him in the completion of such an image,This is what happened in the version of the story included in the biography of Huihai (T#2060, 50: 515c14–19), see above. but he did not. Instead, he first dismissed Wutong, and then granted the image.

In Daoxuan’s text, Wutong asks for the image to be “sent down” (chuijiang 垂降) in an act of grace. By contrast, the stone text does not use the phrase of “sending down” the image, which implies that Sukhāvatī would be located somewhere above our world of humans, a clear contradiction to the description found in the Sukhāvatīvyūha-sūtra .The Sukhāvatīvyūha-sūtra describes Sukhāvatī as a world-system totally independent from our Sahā world, located at an immeasurable distance in the west. Yet Chinese votive inscriptions of the sixth century show that the pure land 净土 of Amitāyus (Wuliangshou Fo 無量壽佛; Buddha Immeasurable Life) was imagined like a heaven 天 of our world-system, comparable to Maitreya’s Tuṣita Heaven. For a detailed analysis of the implications of the term of “sending down the image,” see the respective gloss by Inaba Hiderō 稲葉秀郎 in 4–8. Inaba concludes that when the image of Amitābha and the 50 bodhisattvas circulated under the Sui, there was yet no difference being made between a “heaven” and a “pure land.” Instead, the stone text has the Buddha promise that the image will be “soon bestowed and sent off [to the Sahā world]” (xun qiansong wang 尋遣送往). Thus, the image even acts like an official emissary of Amitābha Buddha, endowed with the power to render efficient vows of rebirth in Amitābha’s Pure Land, as Daoxuan pointed out. In this respect, the auspicious image also resembles the relics of the Buddha, whose general purpose is to continue the Buddha’s presence on earth, almost as an extension of his life-story. 229.

These observations entail the question to which literary genre the text carved at Mount Wolong might belong. So far, it seems to be the sole representative of its kind, as none of the many other depictions of this iconographical subject has an accompanying inscription like the Transmission Report at Mount Wolong. However, the close relationship between the hagiographical aspects of the image and other relics of the Buddha suggests a connection to a text genre that is attested for the first time about three decades later.

The genre in question is exemplified by texts carved on stone steles below depictions of the footprints the historical Buddha is said to have left behind in the city of Pāṭaliputra in the kingdom of Magadha. In his Great Tang Record of Travels to the Western Regions (Da Tang xiyu ji 大唐西域記), Xuanzang relates the legend on how Śākyamuni left impressions of both his feet on a great stone, knowing that they would be venerated a hundred years after his parinirvāṇa by the future king Aśoka.T#2087, 51: 911c10–22; for translations, see 199–200; 毕爾2: 90–91.
The texts that are carved on the steles below the footprints also refer to this legend.

The earliest known sample of such a “Buddha footprint stele” (Fo zu bei 佛足碑) has survived in three fragments that were unearthed at Yuhua Monastery (Yuhua Si 玉華寺) in Yijun County (Yijun Xian 宜君縣), Shaanxi Province. On the fragments, two feet are depicted, with toes turned upwards and soles decorated with auspicious signs. The nearby inscription is titled Report on the Buddha Footprints (Fo ji ji 佛迹記). In the case of the Yuhua Monastery fragment, only the beginning of the text is preserved, which references the footprints on the stone in Pāṭaliputra that “Xuanzang saw and venerated in person 奘親觀禮圖.” Because Xuanzang stayed at Yuhua Monastery from 659–664, it is generally assumed that the footprint stele was carved during that time, and that the image of the footprints is based on a copy of the original Pāṭaliputra footprints that Xuanzang took with him when he returned to China. 654–656.

The Yuhua Monastery footprint stele initiated not only a new iconographical tradition, but also a new literary genre, here called simply “report” (ji 記). The next known footprint stele that claims to be a copy of the original at Pāṭaliputra is the one erected in “the dingmao year of the Hong­wu reign of the great Ming” (大明洪武丁卯), that is, in 1387, by the supervisor (tidian 提點) of Wolong Dhyāna Monastery (Wolong chan Si 卧龍禪寺) in Chang’an, Monk Deming of Hanshui 寒水僧德明. Here, the report below the depiction of the Buddha’s feet has been preserved in its entirety.

The first part of the inscription follows the corresponding passage in Xuanzang’s travelog (T#2087, 51: 911c10–22) rather closely. Other than the travelog version, the stone text mentions that, while still in India, the sacred imprints survived a total of eight attempts to destroy them. The implications of this narrative might be that the Buddha’s footprints waited for Xuanzang’s arrival in India. Xuanzang came in his role as a pilgrim, venerated the footprints, and took a copy back to China to present them to the emperor. Finally, the emperor saw to their wide distribution by imperial decree. The transmission report on the Wolong Dhyāna Monastery stele is followed by a byline, in which Monk Deming documents his order to recarve the footprint stele in 1387.For an illustration see 9: 45(1). For a transcription and translation of the inscribed text see 656–659.

Afterwards, the Wolong Dhyāna Monastery stele was copied several times. The famous Shaolin Monastery (Shaolin Si 少林寺) in Dengfeng 登封, Henan, has a copy erected in 1566,For an illustration and transcription of the carved text, see 9: 46(1); text: 45. and the Aśoka Monastery (Ayu Wang Si 阿育王寺) in Ningbo 寧波, Zhejiang, has another one that was erected in 1899.For an illustration and transcription of the carved text, see 9: 46(3); text: 45. Whenever the footprints of the Buddha were recarved on yet another stele, the transmission report on how the image came to China is continued by another byline documenting the copies that have been taken. In this way, one stop after the other is added to the travel route of the image of the Buddha’s footprints.

We know that copies of Xuanzang’s Pāṭaliputra footprints with accompanying transmission record even reached Korea. There is also evidence that another set of copies of the Pāṭaliputra footprints circulated in China and eventually reached Japan. These were brought from India by the emissary Wang Xuance 王玄策 (622?–682). A sole surviving specimen of Buddha footprints in Yao County (Yao Xian 耀縣) in Shaanxi from the Song dynasty (960–1279) belongs to another iconographical type. The accompanying text narrates that the early pilgrim-monk Faxian 法顯 (320?–420?) copied the Buddha’s footprints he saw in what is modern Sri Lanka, and took them to China. 663–665.

The “Buddha footprint steles” and the hagiographical Transmission Report next to the image in niche 1 at Mount Wolong serve the same purpose, namely, to place copies of an distinguished original icon into a long lineage of transmission from ancient India to China, Korea, and Japan. In medieval China, images such as that of Amitābha Buddha and the 52 Bodhisattvas were thus not only considered manifestations of the sacred, but landmarks encompassing the entire Buddhist world.
